text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
SURVEILLANCE FOLLOW-UP NOTE

PATIENT: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
AGE: 49 years
DIAGNOSIS: High-grade serous carcinoma, bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes, FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0); germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC)
ONCOLOGY VISIT TYPE: Scheduled surveillance follow-up
TIME FROM INITIAL DIAGNOSIS: Approximately 40 months
TIME SINCE nira DISCONTINUATION: 4 months

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 49-year-old woman with a history of FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian cancer with germline BRCA1 mutation who presents for her scheduled surveillance visit, 4 months following planned discontinuation of Zejula (Zejula) maintenance therapy. She reports no new symptoms and is here for interval assessment.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu was initially diagnosed approximately 40 months ago with FIGO IIIC high-grade serous carcinoma of bilateral ovaries and fallopian tubes, with peritoneal carcinomatosis, omental caking, positive ascites, and bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node involvement. Her CA-125 at diagnosis was markedly elevated at 3,847 U/mL. She underwent primary debulking surgery (TAH-BSO, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, peritoneal stripping, and right hemidiaphragm peritoneal implant resection) with complete gross resection achieved (R0). Surgical pathology confirmed pT3c pN1 disease with STIC of the right fallopian tube fimbriated end supporting fallopian tube origin. Germline testing identified a BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC, 5382insC founder mutation), and somatic tumor NGS demonstrated BRCA1 LOH, high genomic loss of heterozygosity (gLOH 31%), and an HRD score of 68.

She subsequently received 6 cycles of adjuvant carbo (AUC 5) and pacli (175 mg/m2, dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 for cycles 5-6 due to cumulative peripheral neuropathy) with a complete biochemical and radiographic response — CA-125 normalized to 11 U/mL and post-treatment CT showed no evidence of disease. Her chemotherapy course was notable for a Grade 2 Paraplatin hypersensitivity reaction during cycle 2 (successfully managed with desensitization protocol), Grade 2-3 peripheral sensory neuropathy, Grade 2 alopecia, and Grade 2 anemia requiring transfusion.

She then initiated nira maintenance therapy at 200 mg daily, which was dose-reduced to 100 mg daily after 3 weeks due to Grade 3 thrombocytopenia (platelets nadir 58,000/uL). She tolerated Zejula 100 mg daily over an extended period with manageable Grade 1-2 thrombocytopenia and hypertension requiring amlodipine. She remained in complete remission throughout her maintenance period, with serial surveillance imaging (at 12 months, 24 months post-chemotherapy) showing no evidence of disease recurrence, and CA-125 persistently normal in the 7-11 U/mL range.

Zejula was discontinued approximately 4 months ago after a total of 28 months of therapy, reflecting shared decision-making discussion regarding cumulative tolerability and the risk-benefit balance at that time point in the context of current SOLO-1 and PRIMA data. The decision was made collaboratively and was consistent with her excellent sustained remission at 36 months from diagnosis.

Since discontinuation of Zejula, the patient reports:
- Platelets have fully recovered, as confirmed on CBC today
- Blood pressure has normalized; amlodipine was successfully tapered and discontinued
- Residual peripheral neuropathy from chemotherapy has largely resolved
- She continues to feel well overall

Today she presents for surveillance. She denies any new pelvic or abdominal pain, bloating, increased abdominal girth, early satiety, change in bowel or bladder habits, vaginal bleeding or discharge, weight loss, or other symptoms of concern. No new fatigue beyond her baseline. No dyspnea or cough. No bone pain.

She notes that her 22-year-old daughter recently underwent genetic testing and was found to be a BRCA1 carrier, and has been referred for risk-reduction counseling. She expresses appropriate concern and emotional processing around this finding.

She also underwent bilateral breast MRI at an outside institution approximately 2 months ago as part of her BRCA1-directed breast surveillance protocol; she reports she was told the scan was normal. The outside report has been requested and is being filed in the chart.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: No fever, chills, or night sweats. No significant fatigue. No weight loss. Appetite intact and stable.
Respiratory: No shortness of breath, cough, hemoptysis, or pleuritic chest pain.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema. Blood pressure has been normal since tapering off amlodipine.
Gastrointestinal: No nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, melena, or hematochezia. No early satiety. No new abdominal distension. Bowel habits regular.
Genitourinary: No urinary frequency, urgency, hematuria, or flank pain. The right renal stone passed spontaneously approximately 10 months ago; no recurrence of flank pain since.
Musculoskeletal: No bone pain, joint pain, or new musculoskeletal complaints.
Neurological: Mild residual tingling in bilateral toes, stable and non-limiting, representing residual Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy from prior PTX. No new neurological symptoms. No headaches.
Skin: Hair fully regrown. No new rashes or skin changes.
Psychiatric: Patient describes her emotional wellbeing as good. She has processed her diagnosis and treatment journey. Anxiety has decreased significantly compared with the initial treatment period. She continues to engage in the oncology support group intermittently.

---

PAST MEDICAL AND SURGICAL HISTORY
(Previously documented; summarized here for completeness)
- High-grade serous carcinoma bilateral ovaries/fallopian tubes FIGO IIIC — as above
- Germline BRCA1 c.5266dupC pathogenic variant
- Paraplatin hypersensitivity (Grade 2, managed with desensitization)
- Drug-induced thrombocytopenia on nira (now off therapy)
- Hypertension (Zejula-associated, now resolved off medication)
- Right renal calculus 4 mm (passed spontaneously, resolved)
- Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (largely resolved)
- Primary debulking surgery (TAH-BSO, omentectomy, appendectomy, lymph node dissection, peritoneal stripping, diaphragmatic stripping)

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Maternal aunt: breast cancer diagnosed age 38
- Maternal grandmother: ovarian cancer diagnosed age 55
- Daughter (age 22): confirmed BRCA1 carrier, referred for risk-reduction counseling

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Secondary school mathematics teacher, returned to full-time teaching
- Non-smoker, minimal alcohol use
- Born in Accra, Ghana; emigrated to U.S. at age 22
- Two children
- Engages in regular physical activity including hiking and yoga
- Participates in oncology support group

ALLERGIES:
- Paraplatin (hypersensitivity — managed with desensitization during chemotherapy)
- No other known drug allergies

CURRENT MEDICATIONS:
- Duloxetine 30 mg daily (for residual chemotherapy-induced neuropathy; patient would like to discuss potential taper given resolution of neuropathy)
- Ondansetron 4 mg PRN nausea (rarely used since stopping nira)
- Multivitamin daily
- Amlodipine: DISCONTINUED approximately 3 months ago (blood pressure normalized off nira)
- Zejula: DISCONTINUED approximately 4 months ago
- Tamsulosin: DISCONTINUED (renal stone passed)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Appears well-nourished and healthy. In good spirits.
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm, respiratory rate 16, temperature 98.4°F, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 65.2 kg (stable, slight increase from treatment nadir weight of ~62 kg; near pre-diagnosis baseline).
HEENT: Normocephalic and atraumatic. Hair fully regrown, good density. No scalp lesions. No scleral icterus. Oral mucosa moist, no ulcers or thrush.
Lymph Nodes: No palpable cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.
Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion. No wheezes or crackles.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No guarding or rebound tenderness. No palpable masses. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated. No fluid wave or shifting dullness to suggest ascites. Well-healed midline abdominal scar from prior laparotomy. No palpable suprapubic mass.
Pelvic (limited external exam): No vaginal vault fullness or tenderness on external assessment. No palpable pelvic masses. (Full pelvic exam deferred given no symptoms; vaginal vault was palpated briefly and unremarkable.)
Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. No joint swelling or tenderness.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation mildly decreased in bilateral toes to light touch (residual Grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy, stable). Deep tendon reflexes 2+ throughout. Gait steady.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CA-125: 11 U/mL (normal, <35 U/mL; sustained in the normal range throughout maintenance and now off maintenance; prior values: 7, 8, 9, 10 U/mL over serial checks)
HE4: Deferred this visit (last checked at post-chemotherapy assessment, normalized at 48 pmol/L)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 5.9 x10^3/uL (normal; previously suppressed on Zejula)
- Hemoglobin: 12.8 g/dL (improved from nadir of 8.9 g/dL during chemotherapy; fully recovered)
- Hematocrit: 38.4%
- Platelets: 198,000/uL (fully recovered to normal range; previously chronically suppressed to 98-128k on Zejula 100 mg; impressive recovery 4 months off drug)
- ANC: 3.6 x10^3/uL

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 139, Potassium 4.0, Chloride 104, CO2 25 (all normal)
- BUN 12, Creatinine 0.80 mg/dL (stable; eGFR >60)
- Total bilirubin 0.5, ALT 18, AST 22, alkaline phosphatase 64 (liver function tests normal)
- Albumin 4.1 g/dL

Blood pressure: 118/74 mmHg (normotensive; amlodipine successfully discontinued)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

The most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis was performed approximately 4 months ago (at the time of the 36-month visit marking planned Zejula completion) — results showed no evidence of disease recurrence; post-hysterectomy/BSO changes, no masses, no ascites, no peritoneal implants, no lymphadenopathy. This represented the patient's fourth consecutive clean scan since completing primary therapy.

Next surveillance CT is scheduled in approximately 2 months (targeting the 42-month timepoint from initial diagnosis), consistent with every-6-month imaging now that she is in the surveillance phase post-maintenance.

Bilateral breast MRI (outside institution, approximately 2 months ago): Per patient report, no suspicious findings identified. Report requested and to be filed. Recommend annual breast MRI and mammogram alternating every 6 months as per BRCA1 high-risk protocol.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN CANCER, FIGO IIIC, GERMLINE BRCA1 — SUSTAINED COMPLETE REMISSION, NOW 40 MONTHS FROM DIAGNOSIS AND 4 MONTHS POST-nira MAINTENANCE

Ms. Osei-Bonsu continues to do extremely well. She is now 40 months out from her initial diagnosis of stage IIIC HGSC with peritoneal carcinomatosis. She achieved an R0 resection at primary debulking, attained a complete biochemical and radiographic response to carbo/pacli, and completed approximately 28 months of nira (Zejula) PARP inhibitor maintenance at a dose-reduced level of 100 mg daily. Serial surveillance imaging has consistently shown no evidence of disease. CA-125 today remains well within the normal range at 11 U/mL, consistent with sustained complete remission.

This is an encouraging trajectory. The combination of germline BRCA1 status, R0 primary debulking, complete biochemical response, and durable remission beyond 3 years confers a meaningfully favorable prognosis relative to unselected stage IIIC patients. I reviewed these data with Ms. Osei-Bonsu in reassuring terms while being clear that ongoing surveillance is important. She understands and is engaged.

Plan for surveillance going forward:
- CA-125 every 3 months x2 years from end of maintenance, then every 6 months thereafter
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 6 months x2 years, then annually — next scan scheduled in approximately 2 months
- If at any point CA-125 rises or she develops new symptoms, we will bring her back sooner and have a low threshold for cross-sectional imaging
- I discussed with her that isolated CA-125 rise without symptoms or imaging correlate in the surveillance setting is common and requires thoughtful interpretation; we would not necessarily act on a single mildly elevated value without confirmatory data

2. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY — RESOLVING/RESOLVED

Residual Grade 1 tingling in bilateral toes from prior Taxol is functionally non-limiting and continues to improve slowly. She is tolerating duloxetine 30 mg daily. Given that neuropathy has improved significantly and is now largely asymptomatic, patient would like to discuss tapering duloxetine. I reviewed the risk and benefit; given she has been on 30 mg (already the starting dose) and symptoms are near-resolved, a slow taper over 4-6 weeks with monitoring is reasonable. She will monitor for any recurrence of neuropathic symptoms or mood changes and contact us if needed. Duloxetine taper discussed and plan initiated; she will reduce to every-other-day dosing for 2-3 weeks, then discontinue. If neuropathy worsens, can restart.

3. THROMBOCYTOPENIA — RESOLVED

Platelets today fully recovered at 198,000/uL, as expected after nira discontinuation. No further intervention required. Patient counseled that platelet recovery is typical and the values may stabilize or even trend slightly higher going forward.

4. HYPERTENSION — RESOLVED

Blood pressure normalized (118/74 mmHg today) with amlodipine discontinuation. No further antihypertensive management required at this time. Will continue to monitor BP at each visit.

5. BRCA1 GERMLINE STATUS — BREAST CANCER SURVEILLANCE AND RISK REDUCTION

Ms. Osei-Bonsu carries a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant and is at significantly elevated lifetime risk for breast cancer in addition to her treated ovarian cancer. She underwent bilateral BSO as part of her debulking surgery, which also provides risk reduction for BRCA1-associated cancer. She has declined risk-reducing mastectomy at this time, which is her informed right, and I support her decision in the context of close surveillance.

Current breast surveillance plan:
- Annual breast MRI — most recent (outside facility, ~2 months ago): reportedly negative; report being filed
- Annual mammography — coordinate with breast surgery/high-risk clinic
- Recommend alternating every-6-months breast surveillance (MRI and mammogram staggered)
- Referral to high-risk breast clinic to ensure coordinated BRCA1-directed surveillance is maintained

Regarding her daughter: I applaud the patient and family for proceeding with genetic testing. Her daughter (age 22) has been confirmed as a BRCA1 carrier and has been referred for genetic counseling and risk-reduction planning. This is an emotionally complex situation for the family, and I provided empathic acknowledgment. I reminded Ms. Osei-Bonsu that her daughter's early identification gives her options for surveillance and risk reduction at a young age, which is ultimately empowering.

6. QUALITY OF LIFE AND PSYCHOSOCIAL WELLBEING

Patient describes her overall wellbeing as good. She has returned to full-time teaching, is physically active with yoga and hiking, and has reconnected with her pre-illness life trajectory. She continues to process the emotional dimensions of her diagnosis and her daughter's BRCA1 status. She attends the oncology support group as needed. No active psychiatric symptoms identified. I encouraged continued engagement with support resources as she finds helpful.

7. FOLLOW-UP

Return visit in approximately 3 months for the next scheduled CA-125 check and clinical assessment. CT imaging in approximately 2 months. Patient given direct contact information and instructed to call if she develops any new symptoms — particularly increased abdominal girth, new pelvic pain, early satiety, unexplained weight loss, or any other concern — and to not wait for her scheduled appointment.

Time spent with patient: approximately 35 minutes, including examination, discussion of test results, counseling, and care coordination. Visit complexity: moderate-high.

Electronically signed,
Attending Gynecologic Oncologist / Medical Oncology
Gynecologic Oncology / Medical Oncology Division
